common_name: Bishop Crown Pepper (Balloon Pepper, Three-Lobed Ornamental South American Chile)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Bishop Crown is a Capsicum baccatum species requiring the same germination conditions as other hot peppers, but Capsicum baccatum species can be slower to germinate than Capsicum annuum — use a heat mat and be patient. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production. The distinctive three-winged crown shape is fully visible from early in the fruit's development and becomes more pronounced as the pepper ripens through yellow to orange to deep red. Harvest at green for mild heat; red ripe for full flavor and slightly higher heat. The thin walls and hollow interior make Bishop Crown unsuitable for thick-walled fresh preparations but excellent for pickling, roasting, and as a garnish where the unusual shape is displayed.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 80–85°F on a heat mat; transplant after last frost. Harvest green for mild use or red for maximum flavor. Bishop Crown (also called Balloon Pepper, Christmas Bell, or Joker's Hat) is a Capsicum baccatum variety named for the distinctive three-pointed crown shape of the fruit — which resembles a bishop's mitre hat from above, or a three-winged balloon when viewed from the side. The fruit has three prominent shoulder lobes that extend outward with a small pendant point below, creating a completely unique silhouette among commonly grown peppers. This shape is genetically characteristic of the Capsicum baccatum species (the Andean peppers, including the ají varieties of South America), where several related varieties share the winged crown form under different names. Bishop Crown fruits measure 2–3 inches in diameter and 1–2 inches in length — small but with a visually complex three-dimensional shape. The walls are thin and crisp, the interior is hollow with minimal placental tissue, and the heat level is mild to moderate (5,000–15,000 Scoville units) — substantially less hot than habanero types but with a fruity, slightly acidic flavor characteristic of the baccatum species. The visual novelty of Bishop Crown makes it one of the most attention-getting peppers in garden displays and at farmers markets, and its mild heat makes it accessible to gardeners who want interesting pepper shapes and flavors without extreme heat levels.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Bishop Crown pepper variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Capsicum baccatum variety collection documentation
Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University — Capsicum baccatum species characteristics

common_name: Peach Habanero Pepper (Pale Peach-Fruity Habanero Variety with Full Heat)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; Peach Habanero, like all Capsicum chinense, requires very warm germination temperatures (80–95°F) and is slow to germinate compared to Capsicum annuum — allow 14–21 days and use a heat mat. In zone 9, transplant March–April; Capsicum chinense thrives in Gulf Coast heat and can produce abundantly through summer and fall. Peach Habanero plants may behave as short-lived perennials in zone 9b if protected from frost, potentially living 2–3 years and producing heavier crops in subsequent seasons. The pale peach color of ripe fruits is the defining characteristic — do not harvest until the green fruits have fully transitioned to the cream-peach color.

directions: Start seeds on heat mat at 85–90°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Harvest when fully peach-colored. Peach Habanero is a color variant of the habanero pepper group (Capsicum chinense) producing fruits that ripen to a distinctive peach to pale apricot-cream color rather than the familiar orange of standard habanero. The heat level is comparable to orange habanero (100,000–350,000 Scoville units) — full habanero-class heat that demands careful handling and measured use — but the flavor character is perceived by many tasters as slightly different: more floral, sweeter, and with a pronounced fruity quality that some describe as reminiscent of apricot or tropical fruit. The habanero group includes color variants ranging from white through yellow, orange, peach, red, chocolate, and nearly black, with all variants sharing the distinctive fruity-floral habanero aroma that distinguishes Capsicum chinense from the more neutral Capsicum annuum species. Peach Habanero is valued by hot sauce makers, hot pepper enthusiasts, and gardeners interested in the visual diversity of the habanero color spectrum — the pale peach fruits are visually striking against the deep green foliage and make an attractive presentation in fresh pepper mixes and hot sauce. In zone 9, Peach Habanero is one of the most productive hot peppers available: the combination of the species' heat and humidity tolerance with the long Gulf Coast growing season produces prolific crops of fruits through summer and into fall.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Peach Habanero pepper variety notes
Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University — Capsicum chinense color variants and heat levels
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Habanero Pepper Production in Florida

common_name: Aleppo Pepper (Halaby Pepper, Syrian Dried Spice Chile with Fruity Mild Heat)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Aleppo pepper is grown primarily as a drying pepper — the fruits are harvested when fully red and ripe, then halved and dried before being crushed into the characteristic coarse flakes with their distinctive oily texture and dark red color. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production. The variety is adapted to hot, dry Mediterranean conditions and performs well in zone 9's summer heat with moderate irrigation. A single productive plant can yield enough peppers for a year's supply of home-ground Aleppo pepper flakes. The drying process is critical to developing the characteristic flavor: Aleppo peppers are traditionally partially dried, then crushed while retaining some moisture and oil content — producing the characteristic slightly moist, aromatic flakes rather than the bone-dry texture of paprika or cayenne.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest when fully red; dry and crush for spice. Aleppo Pepper (also known as Halaby pepper, from Halab, the Arabic name for Aleppo) is a Capsicum annuum variety from Aleppo, Syria — a historic spice and trading city in northern Syria that gave its name to the region's most important spice pepper. Aleppo pepper occupies a unique position in global spice use: with a Scoville rating of approximately 10,000 units, it provides mild to moderate heat (significantly less than cayenne at 30,000–50,000 SHU) combined with a complex, distinctive flavor profile — fruity and slightly sweet, with undertones of cumin and salt, and a characteristic oiliness and depth unlike any other commonly used dried pepper spice. The pepper's distinctive quality comes from the drying and processing method: fruits are split, seeds are removed (seeds are slightly bitter), and the halved peppers are dried partially before being crushed into coarse, slightly moist flakes with a characteristic dark red color and shiny surface from the natural oils retained through partial drying. This traditional processing is quite different from completely desiccated paprika or cayenne, producing a spice with a unique fresh-dried quality. The Syrian civil war that began in 2011 severely disrupted Aleppo pepper production, causing supply shortages and price spikes in global markets; production has since been supplemented by Turkish-grown peppers of similar varieties and by growers in North America who have adopted the variety.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Aleppo pepper variety notes
Kalustyan's Spice Merchants — Aleppo pepper sourcing and historical notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Halaby/Aleppo pepper heirloom preservation documentation

common_name: Rattlesnake Pole Bean (Purple-Streaked Heirloom Snap Bean with Heat Tolerance)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to 65°F; Rattlesnake is a pole bean requiring a trellis, teepee, or fence support 5–6 feet tall. In zone 9, plant April–May and again August for fall production. Rattlesnake's notable heat tolerance — better than many other snap beans — makes it particularly valuable for the Gulf South where spring heats up quickly and bean production from cool-preferring varieties drops sharply. The purple streaks in the pods serve as a visual guide to freshness: pick when the purple streaking is still vivid and the pods snap cleanly. The streaks fade to green when cooked, so the visual distinction is only in the garden and at harvest. Overripe pods become tough and fibrous — harvest frequently (every 2–3 days at peak production) to maintain quality and encourage continued production.

directions: Direct sow 1.5 inches deep after last frost; provide 5–6 foot trellis. Harvest at 7–9 inches when pods snap cleanly. Rattlesnake Pole Bean is an American heirloom snap bean named for the distinctive appearance of its pods: green with irregular purple streaks and mottling that create a pattern reminiscent of rattlesnake skin or scales. This visual character makes Rattlesnake one of the most immediately recognizable heirloom beans in the garden and at market, where the streaked pods attract attention and curiosity. The pods are 7–9 inches long, round in cross-section (round-pod type), and produced in good quantity on vigorous climbing vines. The flavor is rich and full — typical of heirloom pole snap beans, which generally have more complex flavor than modern hybrid bush beans due to the longer maturation time and different genetics of the pole growth habit. Rattlesnake has been noted by growers in hot-climate regions for better-than-average heat tolerance: the variety continues setting pods in temperatures that cause many other snap beans to drop their flowers without producing, making it a practical choice for the Gulf South's warm spring conditions. The seeds (if allowed to dry) are tan with brown mottling — similar to the pod's rattlesnake pattern — and can be saved and grown as a dry bean with a rich, full flavor.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Rattlesnake Pole Bean heirloom variety documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Rattlesnake bean variety notes and history
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heat-tolerant pole bean varieties for the South

common_name: Lazy Wife Pole Bean (Stringless German Heirloom Pole Bean, 1810 American Classic)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Lazy Wife requires a pole or trellis 6–8 feet tall and produces abundantly over a long season. In zone 9, plant April–May; the variety performs adequately but prefers somewhat cooler conditions than the peak Gulf South summer. A late-July or August planting for fall production is often more productive in zone 9 than a late spring planting that encounters July-August heat. The 'lazy' in the variety's name refers not to any person but to the ease of preparation: Lazy Wife is stringless and requires no tedious stringing of the pods before cooking — a significant advantage in the era when most snap beans had tough, fibrous strings running the length of each pod. This stringless quality was unusual and highly valued when the variety was widely grown in the 19th century.

directions: Direct sow 1.5 inches deep after last frost; provide tall support (6–8 feet). Harvest at 5–7 inches as snap bean or leave to dry for shell/dry use. Lazy Wife is a German immigrant heirloom pole bean that was introduced to American seed catalogs around 1810 and became one of the most popular snap beans in the 19th century — a status maintained until modern stringless hybrid varieties provided equivalent ease of preparation with higher yield. The name 'Lazy Wife' refers to the bean's stringless character: in the 19th-century farm kitchen where snap bean preparation required long sessions of stringing pods by hand before cooking, a truly stringless bean required no stringing at all — hence the tongue-in-cheek name suggesting the homemaker could be 'lazy' about bean preparation. The implied humor of the name was accepted in its era; the bean's quality was the real selling point. Lazy Wife produces broad, flat, pale green pods (5–7 inches long, 0.75 inches wide) with a somewhat different form than round-pod types — the flat pod shape and relatively thick walls give Lazy Wife a characteristic visual distinction from most modern snap beans. The pods are truly stringless through their snap-bean stage and have rich, full flavor that heirloom enthusiasts prize. The seeds are creamy-white and excellent as a shell bean (picked young) or dry bean (left to fully mature), giving Lazy Wife three-stage versatility as a snap, shell, or dry bean from a single planting.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Lazy Wife pole bean German heirloom documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Lazy Wife bean historical notes
William Woys Weaver, Heirloom Vegetable Gardening — Historical snap bean variety documentation
